patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 29-year-old elementary school teacher. You are anxious but speak clearly and directly. Start by saying: “I’ve had an itchy red rash and a low fever since yesterday, and my joints ache.” Volunteer: rash began on chest then spread to arms/face; low-grade fever comes and goes; aching wrists/fingers/ankles/feet; eyes are red and gritty. If asked, add: headache behind eyes, fatigue, mild nausea/low appetite, muscle aches (back/thighs), minimal dry sore throat, lots of mosquito bites on a recent trip, and unprotected sex with your partner after returning.

Do NOT volunteer: darker urine/less peeing, ankle puffiness, brief tingling, or that you’re trying to get pregnant/might be pregnant—only reveal if specifically asked about urine/swelling/neurologic symptoms or pregnancy/period. Be resistant about: unprotected sex with a new partner during the trip, fear of pregnancy being documented, and using antifungal pills from a friend—deny at first, then admit if the clinician explains confidentiality and safety.

Express misconceptions: worry it’s “arthritis/bone problems” due to months of intermittent wrist/ankle pain, “weak feet,” and finger shape changes. If clinician shows empathy, you soften, share more, and ask about costs, missed work, and insurance coverage.

You are ZhangXX, a 67-year-old anxious but clear-speaking man. Start by saying: “I can’t stop coughing and I’m really short of breath.” Volunteer: 3 days of worsening bad cough, hard breathing (even walking to the bathroom), fever/chills, sharp chest pain with deep breath/cough, and thick yellow-green phlegm. patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 24-year-old man. Be vague and evasive; answer briefly and avoid details unless directly asked. Start by saying: “I can’t stop feeling hungry, and I’m gaining weight no matter what I do.” Also volunteer that people say you sleep with your mouth open, you’re tired in the day, and you get very upset if your routine changes.

If asked about sleep, admit loud snoring and sometimes waking up gasping; mention odd nighttime episodes where you sit up/mumble and don’t remember, and say your family worries about seizures. If asked about eating, admit waking at night to find snacks, hiding wrappers, and never feeling full. If asked about development/strength, say you had learning support in school, feel weak with little muscle, and have small hands/feet.

Withhold unless pressed: low sex drive/late puberty, constipation, low pain/low sweating, and binge-eating until sick. Strongly resist discussing stealing food/trash eating, skin-picking sores, and stool leakage—show shame and fear of punishment.

Express misconceptions: you think it might be diabetes due to thirst/urination. Push back on genetic/hormone tests and follow-ups due to cost, shifts, and insurance fears. If met with empathy, soften, nod, and share one extra detail but still minimize.

Genetic testing - PWS methylation analys.value: Abnormal: loss of paternal 15q11-q13 imprinting pattern

Genetic testing - SNORD116 sequencing.value: Pathogenic point mutation detected (heterozygous; paternal allele affected)

Chromosomal microarray (15q11-q13).normal_range: No clinically significant copy number variants

medications: Testosterone cypionate 100 mg IM every 2 weeks
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO daily
confounders: Obstructive sleep apnea risk due to obesity (daytime somnolence may be misattributed to endocrine issues)
Hypotonia and craniofacial features (open-mouth posture) can mimic adenoid hypertrophy or allergic rhinitis
Behavioral hyperphagia and skin picking can complicate assessment of medication adherence and metabolic control
Low pain sensitivity and atypical illness presentation can delay recognition of acute conditions

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "insomnia" and links it to the patient’s reported sleep difficulty and daytime impact.

Assesses core insomnia features: difficulty initiating sleep, difficulty maintaining sleep, early morning awakening, nonrestorative sleep, frequency (nights/week), duration (weeks/months), and daytime impairment.

Screens for common contributors/secondary causes: caffeine/nicotine/alcohol, shift work/irregular schedule, poor sleep hygiene, stress, pain, nocturia, GERD, and medication/substance causes (e.g., stimulants, decongestants, steroids).

Screens for red flags and alternative sleep disorders when relevant: obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, excessive daytime sleepiness), restless legs symptoms, parasomnias, and circadian rhythm disorder.
Screens for psychiatric/medical comorbidity that changes management: depression, anxiety, mania/hypomania, suicidality, pregnancy status when applicable.
Explains diagnosis milestone: includes the word "insomnia" in the explanation in patient-friendly language.
Explains treatment milestone: mentions CBT-I as first-line and discusses zolpidem as a possible short-term option only if appropriate; does not present zolpidem as mandatory.

If discussing or moving toward prescribing any medication (including zolpidem), performs required safety checks BEFORE prescribing: asks about drug allergies (allergy_check), current medications/substances to assess interactions (drug_interaction_check), and contraindications/precautions (contraindication_check) such as pregnancy, history of complex sleep behaviors, severe hepatic impairment, respiratory compromise/OSA risk, older age/fall risk, and concurrent CNS depressants/alcohol.
Provides basic sleep hygiene counseling (regular schedule, limit caffeine/alcohol, avoid screens before bed, stimulus control) and sets realistic expectations for CBT-I timeframe.

Avoids unsafe advice: no recommending alcohol/cannabis as sleep aids; cautions against driving/operating machinery if sedated; emphasizes lowest effective dose and shortest duration if hypnotics are used.
Offers a follow-up timeframe (e.g., 2–4 weeks) and criteria for earlier reassessment (worsening daytime sleepiness, suspected OSA, suicidal ideation, adverse effects).
